content.text: is an open-source durable execution engine built for long-running, fault-tolerant workflows. It persists workflow state to a replicated database after every activity, enabling automatic resume after crashes, deploys, rate-limit failures, and timeouts — without writing retry or checkpoint logic yourself.

attrs.alt: Temporal architecture: Client → Frontend/History/Matching Services (persisted to Postgres/CockroachDB) ← polled by Workflow+Activity Workers

content.text:
pip install temporalio

# Run Temporal Server locally (requires Docker)
docker run --rm -p 7233:7233 -p 8080:8080 temporalio/auto-setup

# Temporal Web UI → http://localhost:8080
Docker image bundles the Temporal server and a local SQLite database — suitable for development only. In production, run Temporal Server with an external Postgres or CockroachDB cluster.
all workflow state, event history, timers, and task queues are stored in Postgres, MySQL, or CockroachDB. The server is stateless — you can scale it horizontally.
the Temporal server for tasks — no inbound connections or firewall changes needed. One worker binary can handle both Workflow and Activity tasks.
a named channel that routes tasks to workers. Different services can use different task queues on the same Temporal server.
a tenant-isolation boundary. Separate namespaces for development, staging, and production, with independent retention and auth policies.

import asyncio
from temporalio.client import Client
from temporalio.worker import Worker

async def run_worker():
    client = await Client.connect("localhost:7233")  # Temporal Cloud: "<namespace>.tmprl.cloud:7233"

    worker = Worker(
        client,
        task_queue="ai-agent-queue",
        workflows=[ResearchWorkflow, SummaryWorkflow],  # register all workflow classes
        activities=[
            call_llm,
            write_to_db,
            send_email,
            fetch_url,
        ],  # register all activity functions
    )
    await worker.run()   # blocks, polls Temporal for tasks

asyncio.run(run_worker())
it can execute and then blocks, polling the Temporal server. You can run as many worker processes as needed — they compete for tasks on the same queue, providing horizontal scale.
Activities that run for longer than
periodically to tell Temporal they are still alive. If Temporal stops receiving heartbeats, it cancels and retries the activity.

from temporalio import activity
from datetime import timedelta
import anthropic

@activity.defn
async def summarise_long_document(document_text: str) -> str:
    """Summarise a document that may take >30 seconds."""
    client = anthropic.Anthropic()

    # Send a heartbeat every 10 seconds so Temporal knows the activity is alive.
    # Without heartbeating, Temporal marks a long-running activity as timed out.
    async def heartbeat_loop():
        while True:
            activity.heartbeat("summarisation in progress")
            await asyncio.sleep(10)

    import asyncio
    heartbeat_task = asyncio.create_task(heartbeat_loop())
    try:
        response = client.messages.create(
            model="claude-sonnet-5",
            max_tokens=1000,
            messages=[{"role": "user", "content": f"Summarise:\n\n{document_text}"}],
        )
        return response.content[0].text
    finally:
        heartbeat_task.cancel()
any activity expected to run longer than 30 seconds. Long LLM calls, document processing, and batch HTTP requests all need heartbeating.
is persisted. On retry, the new activity instance can call
to read it — enabling progress resumption within a single activity.
For fan-out patterns — processing a list of items in parallel — spawn one child workflow per item. Each child has its own durable state and retry policy.

). Temporal deduplicates by ID — if the parent crashes and restarts, it re-starts child workflows with the same IDs, which are immediately returned as already-running handles rather than new executions.
re-runs automatically on each cron interval. Temporal schedules the next run immediately after the previous one completes.

from temporalio.client import Client
from datetime import timedelta

async def schedule_daily_report():
    client = await Client.connect("localhost:7233")

    # Start a workflow that re-runs on a cron schedule
    handle = await client.start_workflow(
        DailyReportWorkflow.run,
        args=["sales-data"],
        id="daily-report",
        task_queue="ai-agent-queue",
        cron_schedule="0 9 * * *",    # every day at 09:00 UTC
    )
    print(f"Cron workflow started: {handle.id}")
    # The workflow runs once per cron interval.
    # Temporal automatically re-schedules the next run after each completion.
(a newer API that supports pause, backfill, and jitter) over
if a run is still executing when the next cron fires, the default is to skip the new run. Change to
to queue at most one pending run.
When you change workflow logic, in-flight workflow executions are still replaying their old history. Unguarded code changes cause
to branch between old and new code paths based on the replay version.

from temporalio import workflow

@workflow.defn
class DataProcessingWorkflow:
    @workflow.run
    async def run(self, data: str) -> str:
        # Use get_version to safely evolve workflow logic.
        # Replaying old executions returns the original version number;
        # new executions get the new version — no non-determinism errors.

        version = workflow.get_version(
            "add-validation-step",   # change ID (unique per code change)
            workflow.COMPATIBLE_CHANGE_MIN_SUPPORTED,  # min version
            1,                                         # max version
        )

        if version == workflow.COMPATIBLE_CHANGE_MIN_SUPPORTED:
            # Old path — no validation
            result = await workflow.execute_activity(process, data,
                schedule_to_close_timeout=timedelta(minutes=5))
        else:
            # New path — validate first
            validated = await workflow.execute_activity(validate, data,
                schedule_to_close_timeout=timedelta(minutes=1))
            result = await workflow.execute_activity(process, validated,
                schedule_to_close_timeout=timedelta(minutes=5))
        return result
any change that affects the sequence or result of workflow decisions: adding/removing an activity call, changing activity arguments, reordering steps.
changes to activity logic only (not the workflow function). Activity code can change freely — only the workflow function must be versioned.
once all in-flight executions using the old version have completed, remove the old code path and the
Temporal Cloud is the managed version — Anthropic, Stripe, and Snap use it in production. It eliminates the need to run and scale the Temporal server yourself.

# Connecting to Temporal Cloud (managed service)

from temporalio.client import Client, TLSConfig
import ssl

async def connect_to_cloud():
    # Download your certificates from the Temporal Cloud UI
    with open("/path/to/client.pem", "rb") as f:
        client_cert = f.read()
    with open("/path/to/client.key", "rb") as f:
        client_key = f.read()

    client = await Client.connect(
        "your-namespace.tmprl.cloud:7233",
        namespace="your-namespace",
        tls=TLSConfig(
            client_cert=client_cert,
            client_private_key=client_key,
        ),
    )
    return client

import pytest
from temporalio.testing import WorkflowEnvironment
from temporalio.worker import Worker

@pytest.mark.asyncio
async def test_research_workflow():
    async with await WorkflowEnvironment.start_time_skipping() as env:
        # Time-skipping mode: timers complete instantly — no real sleeping
        async with Worker(
            env.client,
            task_queue="test-queue",
            workflows=[ResearchWorkflow],
            activities=[generate_outline, generate_section],
        ):
            result = await env.client.execute_workflow(
                ResearchWorkflow.run,
                "Vector Databases",
                id="test-research",
                task_queue="test-queue",
            )
        assert "vector" in result.lower()
replaces all timers with instant completions so tests that sleep for 24 hours finish in milliseconds.

(returns the timestamp from the history) instead.
LLM calls on large documents take 60–120 seconds. Without heartbeating, Temporal marks the activity as timed out after
and retries — causing the LLM to be called again from scratch. Always heartbeat activities > 30 s.
class. Calling activity functions directly from another activity bypasses durability entirely.
Temporal stores every event in the workflow history. An unbounded loop that runs thousands of iterations accumulates a history so large it slows replay to a crawl. Use
) to reset history while preserving logical continuity.
a single-node Temporal server is a development setup. Run the History service with multiple replicas and use a replicated database (CockroachDB or Postgres with streaming replication) for production.
(side effects — LLM calls, HTTP, DB). Completed activities are persisted and not re-executed on resume.
Workers poll the Temporal server for tasks — scale by adding more worker processes; no inbound connections or firewall changes required.
long-running activities (> 30 s) so Temporal knows they are alive and can resume within-activity progress on retry.
lets you safely evolve workflow logic without breaking in-flight executions that are still replaying old history.
